results.title: Challenges in Generating Accurate Text in Images: A Benchmark for Text-to-Image Models on Specialized Content
results.content: However, the results revealed that none of the evaluated models—Ideogram, DALL·E 3, and Stable Diffusion—were able to generate text correctly in languages other than English. The models struggled with maintaining the structural and stylistic integrity of non-Latin scripts, leading to illegible characters, distorted symbols, and incorrect text outputs. For scripts such as logographic and abjad, where text complexity is higher, the inaccuracies were even more pronounced. These findings underscore a significant limitation in current text-to-image models, highlighting their lack of cross-lingual rendering capabilities and their inability to handle the unique typographic and spatial requirements of non-Latin languages. [...] Text-to-image (T2I) generative models have emerged as a cornerstone of modern artificial intelligence, enabling the seamless synthesis of high-quality images from textual descriptions. These models have found applications in diverse domains, including creative content generation, educational tools, and assistive technologies. Despite their growing importance, evaluating the performance of T2I models remains a complex and evolving challenge. Standard evaluation metrics, such as Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (CLIP) scores , Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) , and Optical Character Recognition (OCR) accuracy , provide valuable information but often fall short in capturing the nuanced capabilities of these models, particularly for tasks involving intricate textual content. Moreover, [...] The review of existing work highlights three fundamental aspects of text-to-image evaluation: the emerging role of LLMs as evaluators offering enhanced scalability and explainability, the evolution of evaluation frameworks from basic metrics to comprehensive benchmarks addressing compositional and ethical challenges, and the advancement of text rendering through character-aware encoders and glyph-controlled mechanisms. Despite significant progress, challenges persist in achieving reliable evaluation across complex tasks and handling nuanced compositions for specialized applications. These limitations underscore the need for a more systematic approach to assessing text-to-image models, particularly for specialized content generation. results.title: Evaluating Representational Harms in Text-to-Image Models
results.content:
Modeling Languages

# Evaluating Representational Harms in Text-to-Image Models

by Sergio Morales | Jan 27, 2025 | AI, DSLs, requirements, software engineering, tools | 0 comments

Several images generated by Flux Schnell portraying “A poor person” (top) and “A rich person” (bottom), with an evident difference in skin tone representation between the two dimensions. Note that no perceived women are depicted and that ageism is also a clear issue.

Advances in online Text-to-Image (T2I) generator models allow users and organizations alike to generate millions of images from text prompts. Unfortunately, even elementary prompts result in displaying noticeable social biases in the models’ output imagery. [...] The following listing contains excerpts of the ethical concern and dimension datasets. Given these values, a template like “A {DIMENSION} {GENDER}” would be instantiated as “A seductive man”, “A seductive woman”, “A powerful man”, “A powerful woman”, and so on.

ImageBiTe is prepared to check OpenAI’s, HuggingFace’s and many other T2I models available online. Similarly, our tool can use any vision model from OpenAI or available in Hugging Face to inspect the generated images and assist in the distribution calculation.

## Conclusions and Future Work [...] The distorted or demeaning misconception of social groups caused by T2I outputs is known as representational harm [1-2], and they are sadly usual. There are many examples of CEOs and other job occupations usually portrayed as white men [3–5], and users getting pornographic results for “black girls” but not “white girls” from search engines . Bloomberg published a report disclosing race and gender stereotypes in images generated by Stable Diffusion.

results.title: Text-to-image Diffusion Models in Generative AI: A Survey
results.content:
### 7.2 Outlook

Safe and fair applications. With the wide application of text-to-image models, how to mitigate the ethical issues and security risks of current text-to-image models is demanding and challenging. Possible directions include a more diverse and balanced dataset to mitigate issues like race and gender, advanced methods for detecting generated images, and robust diffusion models against various attacks. [...] Misuse for malicious purposes. Text-to-image diffusion models have shown their power in generating high-quality images. However, this also raises great concern that the generated images may be used for malicious purposes, e.g., falsifying electronic evidence . DE-FAKE  is the first to conduct a systematical study on visual forgeries of the text-to-image diffusion models, which aims to distinguish generated images from the real ones, and also further track the source model of each fake image.
To achieve these two goals, DE-FAKE  analyzes from visual modality perspective, and finds that images generated by different diffusion models share common features and also present unique model-wise fingerprints. [...] Security and privacy risks. While text-to-image diffusion models have attracted great attention, the security and privacy risks have been neglected so far. Two pioneering works [72, 73] discuss the backdoor attack and privacy issues, respectively. Inspired by the findings in

results.title: A Survey of Personalizable Text to Image Diffusion Models
results.content:
4.2 Compositionality A common problem with base models such as Dreambooth, Textual Inversion, and Custom Diffusion are that they struggle with compositionality. Generated images may not respect spatial relationships ("A is on the left of B"), miscount objects, mix up colors between objects, or otherwise confuse subject attributes. This is particularly an issue in personalizable models and even more so when multiple personalized concepts are present. Figure 3 demonstrates how attributes between subjects can be confused by models like DreamBooth. While the prompt speciﬁes that there should be both a cat and dog, only a dog is generated. Additionally, note that the attributes of the cat and dog are confused and applied to both subjects, resulting in inconstent identity of the uniquely [...] survey of advancements, organized into the following categories of conditions: generic concepts, people, interactions, and layouts. Finally, we discuss outstanding problems with existing models and future directions for work. [...] Dani Valevski, Danny Wasserman, Yossi Matias, and Yaniv Leviathan. results.title: Introduction to the Structure and Mathematics of Text-to-Image Models
results.content:
This section introduces various text-to-image models, detailing their architectures and methodologies. Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) involve a dual network system for image generation and validation. Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) utilize a probabilistic approach to encode and decode images. Transformer-based models, like DALL-E, leverage transformer networks for efficient text-to-image translation. Diffusion models, such as GLIDE, generate images through a controlled process of adding and removing noise. Lastly, Autoregressive Models create images in a sequential manner, pixel by pixel. Each model’s unique approach to processing text embeddings, image generation techniques, and training mechanisms are explored, providing an understanding of their contributions to the field of [...] The most common loss function used in autoregressive models is cross-entropy loss, calculated between the predicted pixel values and the actual pixel values of the training images. This loss ensures that each pixel is accurately predicted, given the preceding pixels and the text description.

## Conclusion [...] ## Conclusion

This blog post has provided an overview of text-to-image models, highlighting the structural and mathematical details that define their operation. From processing text to generating images and the complexities of training and evaluation, these models showcase the interaction between various AI disciplines. Despite the challenges and limitations, the continuous evolution in their architectures and methodologies not only enhances our understanding of AI-driven image synthesis but also opens up new avenues for practical applications.
